skill.name: port-adapter-designer
skill.description: Designs Rust port traits and adapter implementations for external systems in hexagonal architecture.
skill.summary: Create clean Rust ports and adapters for databases, APIs, notifications, and tests.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged command execution, network, environment access, weak cryptography, and obfuscation patterns. Review found these are false positives from Markdown fenced Rust examples and ordinary architecture terminology, not executable skill behavior. No prompt injection or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Rust Code Fences Matched as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported backtick execution locations are Markdown fenced Rust examples for trait and adapter design. The skill file grants only Read and Grep tools, and no executable script or shell invocation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched regions are fenced Rust snippets, not Ruby or shell backtick execution. They define traits and structs and do not instruct the assistant to run commands.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Example Payment API Call
low_findings.description: The hardcoded Stripe URL appears inside an illustrative HTTP adapter example. It does not cause the skill to make a network request and is used to show how an adapter can wrap an external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network URL and bearer authentication are inside a Rust example block. No tool permission or instruction exists for the skill itself to contact that endpoint.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Placeholder API Key Field
low_findings.description: The flagged API key references are an example struct field and bearer authentication call in a payment adapter. No environment variable is read, and no secret value is embedded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses a field named api_key as a placeholder in a sample adapter. There is no process environment access, credential harvesting, or exfiltration logic.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Heuristic
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections correspond to prose and Markdown structure, not cryptographic code. No hash function, encryption routine, or security-sensitive algorithm is implemented.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic API usage at the flagged lines. The matches appear to be substring or heuristic artifacts from Markdown content.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Obfuscation Combination Heuristic
low_findings.description: The obfuscation and dangerous-combination findings are caused by Markdown delimiters plus example code that includes an HTTP client and an API key field. The file is readable documentation and contains no obfuscated payload.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill content is plain Markdown with normal Rust examples. The combined heuristic has no semantic support because no executable code, network tool use, or credential collection instruction exists.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Heuristic
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance line is part of a SQL query example selecting user fields. It does not inspect the host system, enumerate files, or collect environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a static SQL string in documentation. It targets an application table in an example repository adapter, not the user's machine.

content.value_statement: External services can leak infrastructure concerns into Rust domain code. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design clear ports, adapters, and test doubles.
actual_capabilities: Designs Rust trait ports for repositories, payment gateways, and notification services.
Suggests adapter structures for PostgreSQL, HTTP clients, and in-memory testing.
Shows async trait signatures using domain types and domain errors.
Recommends dependency boundaries for hexagonal architecture.
Explains how to replace external systems with test adapters.
limitations: It provides design guidance and examples, not complete production integrations.
It does not verify database schemas, API contracts, or payment provider requirements.
It assumes Rust projects can use async traits and dependency injection patterns.
It does not run tests or inspect code beyond the tools available to the assistant.

use_cases.title: Separate Domain Logic from Persistence
use_cases.description: Design repository traits and database adapters so domain services do not depend on SQL clients.

use_cases.title: Wrap External APIs Behind Ports
use_cases.description: Create service ports for payment, messaging, or notification providers before choosing an adapter implementation.

use_cases.title: Improve Testability with In-Memory Adapters
use_cases.description: Replace live databases and services with simple adapters for unit and integration tests.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Ports
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust module and identify external dependencies that should become ports. Explain each suggested port in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Repository Port
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Rust repository port for this aggregate. Use domain types, async methods, and domain-level error names.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Adapter
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an adapter design for this external API. Include constructor dependencies, error mapping, and test strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Toward Hexagonal Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Rust service and propose a port-adapter refactor plan. Include trait boundaries, adapter modules, and migration steps.

output_examples.input: I have a Rust service that calls a database client directly.
output_examples.output: The skill would identify persistence as an external dependency, propose a repository port, and describe a database adapter that maps database errors into domain errors.

output_examples.input: I need to integrate a payment provider without coupling my domain code.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest a payment gateway port, list the needed operations, and describe an HTTP adapter with authentication and response mapping.

output_examples.input: My tests should not require a live external service.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend an in-memory or mock adapter and explain how to inject it into the service under test.

best_practices: Keep port methods focused on domain needs instead of provider-specific operations.
Use domain types and domain errors at every port boundary.
Create test adapters before adding complex infrastructure adapters.
anti_patterns: Do not expose database rows or HTTP response objects through domain ports.
Do not create large ports that combine unrelated external services.
Do not let adapters decide business rules that belong in the domain.

faq.question: What architecture style does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports hexagonal architecture and ports-and-adapters design for Rust applications.

faq.question: Does it generate complete working adapters?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and design guidance. Production adapters still need project-specific types, dependencies, and tests.

faq.question: Can it help with database repositories?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes repository port patterns and PostgreSQL adapter examples.

faq.question: Can it help with HTTP APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to wrap external HTTP clients behind service ports.

faq.question: Is it specific to Rust?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples and recommendations are written for Rust and async trait patterns.

faq.question: Can it improve testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends in-memory adapters and dependency injection to avoid live external systems in tests.
